segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: We've been talking about the law of inertia.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: An object moving in a straight line will continue to move in a straight line unless it is acted upon by another force.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: So what does this mean in the real world?

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Let's look at something many people do all the time, riding a bicycle.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Think about riding along in a straight line.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Once the bike is moving, inertia will keep it going in a straight line.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: But we don't just ride in straight lines, do we?

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: We turn at corners and steer around things that are in our way.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: So how do you get the bicycle not to go straight?

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Don't you just need to turn the front wheel in the direction you want to turn?

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: In fact, if you only turn the front wheel, you're likely to end up on the pavement.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: The wheel will turn, but because of inertia, your body and the rest of the bike will want to keep moving straight ahead.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: So how does a cyclist avoid this?

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: The key is to lean in.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Weight needs to shift in the direction of the turn, so that the force of gravity can work against the force of inertia.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Professional cyclists who move at high speeds need to hit a sweet spot.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: They must lean more to keep their bicycles from sliding out of turns, but not too much.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: At a certain point, gravity will bring both rider and bike down to the ground.
